title: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
subtitle: SICP — The Wizard Book
author: Harold Abelson, Gerald Jay Sussman, Julie Sussman
themes: Building abstractions with procedures — lambda calculus, recursion, higher-order functions
Building abstractions with data — compound data, symbolic data, generic operations
Modularity, objects, and state — mutable data, streams, concurrency
Metalinguistic abstraction — metacircular evaluator, lazy evaluation, nondeterministic computing, logic programming
Computing with register machines — explicit-control evaluator, compilation, garbage collection
awards: ACM SIGSOFT Influential Educator Award (Abelson & Sussman, 2011)
Widely regarded as one of the most important CS textbooks ever written
Foreword by Alan J. Perlis (first Turing Award winner)
Used as MIT's introductory CS textbook (6.001) from 1984–2007
Creative Commons BY-SA 4.0 licensed (freely available online)
excerpt: The Wizard Book. MIT's legendary 6.001 textbook that teaches not a programming language, but the very nature of computation — from lambda calculus to metacircular evaluators to compilers. Scheme as the vehicle, abstraction as the destination.
